title: The Matrix of a Linear Transformation

content.title: Matrix Representation
content.content:
Let $V$ and $W$ be finite-dimensional vector spaces with ordered bases 
$\mathcal{B} = (v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ and $\mathcal{C} = (w_1, \ldots, w_m)$ 
respectively. Let $T: V \to W$ be linear.

The **matrix of $T$ with respect to $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$**, 
denoted $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}}$ or $\mathcal{M}(T, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C})$, 
is the $m \times n$ matrix whose $j$-th column consists of the coordinates 
of $T(v_j)$ with respect to $\mathcal{C}$:
\[
T(v_j) = \sum_{i=1}^m a_{ij} w_i \implies [T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}} = (a_{ij})
\]
When $V = W$ and $\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{C}$, we write simply $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$.

content.title: Matrix-Vector Multiplication
content.content:
Let $T: V \to W$ be linear with matrix $A = [T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}}$. 
If $v \in V$ has coordinate vector $[v]_{\mathcal{B}} = x \in \FF^n$, then
\[
[T(v)]_{\mathcal{C}} = A \cdot x
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $v = \sum_{j=1}^n x_j v_j$ and $T(v_j) = \sum_{i=1}^m a_{ij} w_i$. Then
\[
T(v) = T\left(\sum_j x_j v_j\right) = \sum_j x_j T(v_j) = \sum_j x_j \sum_i a_{ij} w_i = \sum_i \left(\sum_j a_{ij} x_j\right) w_i
\]
The coefficient of $w_i$ is $\sum_j a_{ij} x_j = (Ax)_i$.

content.title: Composition and Matrix Multiplication
content.content:
Let $S: U \to V$ and $T: V \to W$ be linear maps. Then
\[
[T \circ S]_{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathcal{C}} = [T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}} \cdot [S]_{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathcal{B}}
\]
where $\mathcal{A}$, $\mathcal{B}$, $\mathcal{C}$ are bases for $U$, $V$, $W$ respectively.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For any $u \in U$:
\[
[(T \circ S)(u)]_{\mathcal{C}} = [T(S(u))]_{\mathcal{C}} = [T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}} [S(u)]_{\mathcal{B}} = [T]_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{C}} [S]_{\mathcal{A}}^{\mathcal{B}} [u]_{\mathcal{A}}
\]
Since this holds for all $u$, the matrices are equal.

content.title: Change of Basis Matrix
content.content:
Let $\mathcal{B} = (v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ and $\mathcal{B}' = (v_1', \ldots, v_n')$ 
be two bases for $V$. The **change of basis matrix** from 
$\mathcal{B}'$ to $\mathcal{B}$, denoted $P_{\mathcal{B}' \to \mathcal{B}}$, 
is the $n \times n$ matrix whose $j$-th column is $[v_j']_{\mathcal{B}}$.

That is, if $v_j' = \sum_{i=1}^n p_{ij} v_i$, then $P = (p_{ij})$.

content.title: Coordinate Transformation
content.content:
If $P = P_{\mathcal{B}' \to \mathcal{B}}$, then for any $v \in V$:
\[
[v]_{\mathcal{B}} = P \cdot [v]_{\mathcal{B}'}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $[v]_{\mathcal{B}'} = (x_1', \ldots, x_n')^T$, so $v = \sum_j x_j' v_j'$. 
Substituting $v_j' = \sum_i p_{ij} v_i$:
\[
v = \sum_j x_j' \sum_i p_{ij} v_i = \sum_i \left(\sum_j p_{ij} x_j'\right) v_i
\]
Thus $[v]_{\mathcal{B}} = P \cdot [v]_{\mathcal{B}'}$.

content.title: Change of Basis for Operators
content.content:
Let $T: V \to V$ be a linear operator. If $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{B}'$ 
are bases for $V$ with change of basis matrix $P = P_{\mathcal{B}' \to \mathcal{B}}$, then
\[
[T]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1} [T]_{\mathcal{B}} P
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $A = [T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ and $A' = [T]_{\mathcal{B}'}$. For any $v \in V$:
$$
A' [v]_{\mathcal{B}'} &= [T(v)]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1} [T(v)]_{\mathcal{B}} \\
&= P^{-1} A [v]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1} A P [v]_{\mathcal{B}'}
$$
Since this holds for all $v$, we have $A' = P^{-1} A P$.

content.title: Similar Matrices
content.content:
Two $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$ are **similar** 
if there exists an invertible matrix $P$ such that $B = P^{-1} A P$.

We write $A \sim B$ to indicate that $A$ and $B$ are similar.

content.title: Similarity is an Equivalence Relation
content.content:
Similarity is an equivalence relation on $M_n(\FF)$:

1. Reflexive: $A \sim A$

2. Symmetric: $A \sim B$ implies $B \sim A$

3. Transitive: $A \sim B$ and $B \sim C$ implies $A \sim C$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Invariants of Similarity
content.content:
If $A \sim B$, then:

1. $\tr(A) = \tr(B)$

2. $\det(A) = \det(B)$

3. $\rank(A) = \rank(B)$

4. $A$ and $B$ have the same characteristic polynomial

5. $A$ and $B$ have the same eigenvalues (with multiplicities)
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $B = P^{-1} A P$.

(a) $\tr(B) = \tr(P^{-1} A P) = \tr(A P P^{-1}) = \tr(A)$ using 
$\tr(XY) = \tr(YX)$.

(b) $\det(B) = \det(P^{-1}) \det(A) \det(P) = \det(A)$.

(c) The linear maps represented by $A$ and $B$ have the same image 
dimension.

(d) $\det(B - \lambda I) = \det(P^{-1}(A - \lambda I)P) = \det(A - \lambda I)$.

(e) Follows from (d).

content.title: Computing $P_{\mathcal{B}' \to \mathcal{B}}$
content.content:
To find the change of basis matrix from $\mathcal{B}' = (v_1', \ldots, v_n')$ 
to $\mathcal{B} = (v_1, \ldots, v_n)$:

1. Form the augmented matrix $[v_1 | v_2 | \cdots | v_n \mid v_1' | v_2' | \cdots | v_n']$

2. Row reduce the left half to the identity

3. The right half becomes $P$

content.title: Change of Basis in $\RR^2$
content.problem:
Let $\mathcal{B} = ((1,0), (0,1))$ (standard) and 
$\mathcal{B}' = ((1,1), (1,-1))$.

The change of basis matrix $P$ from $\mathcal{B}'$ to $\mathcal{B}$ has 
columns $[v_1']_{\mathcal{B}} = (1,1)^T$ and $[v_2']_{\mathcal{B}} = (1,-1)^T$:
\[
P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
\]

content.title: Diagonalization Criterion
content.content:
An operator $T: V \to V$ is **diagonalizable** 
(i.e., $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ is diagonal for some basis $\mathcal{B}$) if 
and only if $V$ has a basis of eigenvectors of $T$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
($\Rightarrow$) If $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \diag(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$, 
then $T(v_i) = \lambda_i v_i$, so each $v_i$ is an eigenvector.

($\Leftarrow$) If $(v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is a basis of eigenvectors with 
$T(v_i) = \lambda_i v_i$, then $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \diag(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$.
